skill.name: customer-research
skill.description: Guides customer research by analyzing interviews, surveys, reviews, support tickets, and online community discussions to find customer pains, jobs, triggers, and voice-of-customer language.
skill.summary: Analyze and synthesize customer research into themes, quotes, personas, and research-informed recommendations.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples, file extensions, search operators, and documentation links rather than executable code. No prompt injection, credential access, command execution, malware behavior, or data exfiltration intent was found in the reviewed files. The skill is publishable with low risk because it guides web research and may read a local product marketing context file when present.

low_findings.title: Benign Local Context File Read
low_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant to read product marketing context files if they exist. This is scoped to named local Markdown files and is intended to avoid repeated questions, but users should know local context may influence the research workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and limited to two named Markdown files. I found no evidence that it reads arbitrary paths or sends file contents elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Online Research Guidance Uses External Sources
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to gather research from online sources such as Reddit, G2, forums, and review sites. This is expected behavior for a customer research skill and does not include automated network requests or hidden exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external-source behavior is central to the skill description and appears as user-directed research guidance. No code or instruction sends private data to a third-party endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed locations are inline examples, fenced templates, related skill names, tags, or search operators, not executable Ruby code or shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visibly Markdown documentation. There is no script file, command runner, interpreter call, or user-controlled command construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto Pattern Matches Text and File Extensions
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns across SKILL.md, references/source-guides.md, and evals/evals.json. Review found ordinary text such as Markdown file names, research terminology, and expected-output strings rather than cryptographic code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are Markdown guidance and JSON eval fixtures. I found no hashing, encryption, authentication, or cryptographic API usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Relative Documentation Link Reported as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: The path traversal alert points to a Markdown link that references another documentation file. It is not used in code to read files and does not accept user-controlled path input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The ../../../ sequence appears only inside a documentation hyperlink. No filesystem API call or executable path resolution is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms Are Research Source Lists
dangerous_patterns.description: Static reconnaissance alerts correspond to lists of communities, review sites, and research sources used for customer discovery. They do not instruct scanning networks, probing hosts, or collecting system information.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context is market and customer research, not technical reconnaissance. No evidence found of host discovery, port scanning, or environment enumeration.

content.value_statement: Teams often have interviews, reviews, and support tickets but lack a clear way to turn them into decisions. This skill structures customer evidence into themes, quotes, personas, jobs, and research gaps.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes customer interviews, surveys, support tickets, NPS comments, and review data.
Extracts jobs to be done, pain points, triggers, desired outcomes, objections, and alternatives.
Guides research from Reddit, G2, Capterra, forums, app stores, LinkedIn, and other public sources.
Builds research-backed personas only when enough source evidence exists.
Produces synthesis reports, VOC quote banks, JTBD maps, competitive summaries, and gap analyses.
Labels insights by confidence using sample size, source quality, recency, and consistency.
limitations: It depends on the quality, recency, and relevance of the source material provided.
It should not invent persona details when research evidence is missing.
It does not replace direct customer interviews or statistically valid survey design.
Online community research may overrepresent vocal, technical, or dissatisfied users.

use_cases.title: Synthesize Interview Transcripts
use_cases.description: Turn customer interviews into ranked themes, evidence-backed insights, and representative quotes for messaging or product decisions.

use_cases.title: Mine Reviews and Communities
use_cases.description: Study Reddit threads, G2 reviews, app store comments, and forums to find pains, alternatives, and buyer language.

use_cases.title: Build Evidence-Based Personas
use_cases.description: Create practical personas from confirmed patterns across interviews, reviews, tickets, and public research sources.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Existing Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: the user has raw interview, survey, or ticket data.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have customer research notes and want to understand the main themes. Help me extract pains, jobs, triggers, outcomes, and useful quotes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Online Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: the user has no interviews and needs public sources.
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to research buyers for [product category]. Suggest the best online sources, search queries, and fields to capture for each source.

prompt_templates.title: Create a VOC Quote Bank
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: the user wants customer language for later messaging work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Organize these customer quotes into a voice-of-customer quote bank by pain, trigger, desired outcome, objection, and alternative.

prompt_templates.title: Synthesize Competitive Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: the user needs a structured competitor insight report.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze reviews and community posts about these competitors. Rank themes by frequency and intensity, cite representative quotes, and identify positioning opportunities.

output_examples.input: Six customer interviews about why teams switched from spreadsheets to a reporting tool.
output_examples.output: A synthesis report with top themes, confidence labels, trigger events, buyer language, representative quotes, and messaging implications.

output_examples.input: G2 reviews and Reddit threads about project management software complaints.
output_examples.output: A competitive insight summary covering recurring frustrations, switching triggers, unmet needs, alternatives considered, and source-backed quotes.

output_examples.input: Support tickets and NPS comments from churned customers.
output_examples.output: A churn research brief that separates bugs, confusion, missing features, expectation gaps, and recommended follow-up questions.

best_practices: Start by defining the research goal and target segment before analyzing sources.
Keep verbatim quotes tied to source, date, context, and confidence level.
Separate strong evidence from hypotheses, and mark gaps that need more research.
anti_patterns: Do not average across different customer segments or churn reasons.
Do not build personas from assumptions or a single anecdote.
Do not treat online reviews as fully representative of the broader market.

faq.question: What inputs work best with this skill?
faq.answer: Customer interviews, surveys, support tickets, NPS verbatims, reviews, forum posts, and community discussions work well.

faq.question: Can it help when I have no customer interviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It can plan digital watering hole research using public sources such as Reddit, G2, forums, and app store reviews.

faq.question: Does it create customer personas?
faq.answer: Yes, but it should create personas only after enough evidence exists for a consistent customer segment.

faq.question: How does it handle weak evidence?
faq.answer: It labels insights by confidence and separates confirmed patterns from hypotheses or open research questions.

faq.question: Can it write marketing copy from the research?
faq.answer: It prepares voice-of-customer themes and quote banks, then recommends using a copywriting workflow for final copy.

faq.question: Is web access required?
faq.answer: Web access is only needed when gathering new public research. Existing transcripts or exports can be analyzed without browsing.
